sentences.text: Radio was a-- was a-- newspapers, we had local newspapers.
We have the Shanghai English-speaking newspapers.
We had some small newspapers on our own local community.
Really, I would say that we were probably better informed what was going on in Europe than people in the-- in other parts of the-- many other parts of the world.
I should say.
I don't know about the neutral parts of the world.
Because he has, till 19-- the end of 19-- till the war in the Pacific broke out, the first two years, we had news in various languages.
We had-- the Germans were there.
The French were there.
The English were there.
The American were there.
The-- I don't know if the name [?
Carl ?] Alcott means anything to you.
Carl ?] Alcott was the news commentator, and he didn't sound like an old man then like late-- like later here.
And there was an American radio station XMHA and he was one-- one of our favorite communicators or-- we listened to that was on the English station.
There was somebody, Peter Adams.
I-- some place, I heard his real name.
But I don't know.
They-- they-- of course, they took him out before the-- before-- when the British embassy and when they all left.
But-- and we listened to him.
He was speaking in German.
And the American broadcasting station, I really don't know if they had a German broadcast or not, not at that time.
I-- I don't think so.
But [?
Carl ?] Alcott, we listened to.
And what-- what English-- most of us knew enough English to at least follow it.
And, of course, if you wanted to hear the other side, there was a German.
And you wanted to hear a third side, there were the Russians, which all was broadcast in English, and I believe in German too at one time.
I can't swear by that-- certainly in English.
And then there were the Japanese stations who also had English broadcast.
So I think we got a cross cut.
And strangely enough, most of them weren't lying too much.
It wouldn't have made sense because there was-- now, the commentary, it was different.
I'm talking about the news reporting directly.
Now, the commentary was of course-- the German commentary was certainly different than the commentary you heard from English American stations even-- even though America was not involved.
But I-- I would say we were well informed what was going on.
What we were not not too well-informed was about the concentration camp, and what's going on in-- in Germany and in Poland.
We were not too well-informed of that.
There were rumors only.
We were not informed.
But about the general world we were.
Because of the competition, they were all in there.
You see, we were not the only listeners there.
There were about 3,000 to 4,000 Germans living there.
There were about 25,000 Russians living there.
There were about 4,000 Russian Jews living there.
So-- and-- and and there were a number of thousands at that time still American and British subjects living there.
So-- and French people, by all means.
They had their own concessions there.
And they a fantastic-- I-- I forgot his name-- fantastic narrator on the radio, also in German.
And so we got a cross cut.
And to say we were not informed is totally misleading.
We heard what was going on in the war.
We knew pretty well what was going on.
Like I said, we did not hear what the Germans did in-- in their area.
Because they didn't say it and the others didn't either.
I have on tape a-- a-- somebody on-- on a tape I have where he said he knew-- his family knew a Japanese officer where he befriended them.
And one day, the Japanese officer called him as a 14-year-old boy or something.
And he said, do you know what they're doing to your people?
And he told him about the Holocaust.
This must have been around probably '41 or something.
So he went to his rabbi, tell him.
And the rabbi threw him out for telling stories like this.
Nobody wanted to believe it.
That's what he said on television.
I can't vouch for any of that, but it's possible.
The rumors we heard-- rumors we heard that the Jews were all-- were killed and so on.
But these were rumors and vague rumors and nothing definite as far as I recall.
Somebody else may have known more.
Yes, absolutely.
We even had our own services.
And they were there.
They were-- there were at least three large synagogues in Shanghai at the time.
One was a Sephardic one, two were Ashkenazic synagogues.
They built [? these ?] right around that time a new-- a new-- a new Askenazic, a big synagogue.
So those were Russian Jewish were there for 20 years or close to 20 years and did quite well, most of them.
Most of them were quite well off or made a good living.
And what I didn't know is that-- again, this is from another tape-- that most of them, and they requested it, were given Soviet citizenship.
Because they felt they may have been threatened by the Japanese, they gave them Soviet citizenship.
Now, I won't vouch for that.
This is strictly what I've-- I've seen on the tape.
A Russian-- seemed to me a Russian Jewish woman later on testifying to it.
Absolutely possible.
Because I-- my-- I later on worked for a small shop who hired basically only refugees.
He had a couple of Chinese too.
He did-- he did all kinds of work, turning machine over to foot pedal, wood-burning stove, he made.
It's ovens.
Kerosene stoves they built, and also machines, certain machines like [? canning ?] machines and so on.
And then at the time he had-- he had some customers that were Russian.
Some of them were Jewish.
And they said these people are Soviet citizens.
And that-- that I remember.
But they were also only refugees, really.
But they-- but they evidently, they were-- they was able.
I did not know this until I saw that tape quite recently that-- that supposedly, all of them became Soviet citizens.
Wanna know-- ?] the Japanese did not touch them.
They would never say Jew.
They would say-- when they-- their proclamation on, I think, in-- in in early '43 which forced us to live from May 18, I think, to-- '43 to September 9 or 3, 1945.
Their proclamation only talked about stateless refugees from central Europe.
I have some tapes.
A friend of mine put some tapes together-- from-- took from other tapes.
He had all kinds of tapes.
He put it on one tape.
It is about Shanghai, about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] refugees like us, all from a-- Russian.
There's quite a bit in there.
How did he get the tapes?
I-- I do not know.
He made it a hobby.
He had a whole room full of books about China, Shanghai.
He made it a hobby.
He's-- he's semi-retired and that-- that is this hobby.
And he-- and he-- and he said, I saw one of the tape that at last [INAUDIBLE] have the picture from, one of them.
And he said, I-- I get you one.
And then he gave me-- he make copies of other tapes.
It's-- it's technically poor.
His name is-- is Martin Moses.
Only in the night school.
I took all kinds of things in night school.
First of all, for the trade, I went to night school, technical classes, which we got.
And they were not bad at all.
We had people there, particularly people from Austria, some people that were engineers that were very qualified to teach.
But they had also a school they called the [?
Forum, ?] which the Jewish community center or [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they called it.
It-- it's like a federation here.
And they-- there was a private man.
He started it.
When the war broke out in the Pacific and a lot of-- number of Jewish people from central Europe that had money went to university in Shanghai.
And they couldn't go anymore.
And they had to live in the [? other ?] area, what they call the ghetto-- restricted area.
And he started them.
And then he had a-- he was a librarian, really.
He started a little school called [?
Forum ?] where they had speeches.
They have retired professors from all kinds of fields there.
Those were-- some of them taught in German because their English was never good enough.
Some of them did actually teach in Shanghai.
And they were all forced to live there.
They all lost their jobs.
So they-- so we had a night-- what they called [?
Forum. ?] And then the Jewish federation took it over on a little bigger scale.
And we had-- we had very fantastic-- one was music appreciation taught by a man who was actually a professor of economy.
[LAUGHS] And he predicted the economy of the future.
Boy, was he wrong [LAUGHS] in [? his own ?] way.
Then we had literature, professor of literature.
We had a history professor and even some of the students who were holding speeches.
And we had-- we had-- we had discussions, and it was highly interesting.
It was actually-- that was every bit on a college level.
And a friend of mine introduced me to that.
And I loved it.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, in-- in the downtown.
Not too many, but you still-- you could see them.
There were sometimes Nazi flags that were visible.
I can't remember.
I can't remember having seen any in uniform.
But the Germans did send somebody there.
And I cannot-- I don't know his name.
They sent somebody there to make sure that the Germans wouldn't have any business with us.
But in truth, he himself had business with the refugees.
He was seen in the restaurants.
He was talking to people.
They knew who he was.
He knew who we were.
And-- and the German who did have business with the Jews didn't give a damn, excuse my expression.
They obviously didn't.
They were not exactly the same as the Nazis in Germany.
I worked in a German house once, and I needed a special passport to get there and stayed.
I think I saw some women there.
I don't know who they were.
There-- there were about a dozen servants in the house.
And what they did was repairing the locks.
All the locks needed some repairing and serving.
So another fellow and I.
And they hired.
They hired.
My boss was Jewish.
How he has the connections, well, that I don't know.
That's another story.
He had all kinds of connection.
Well, worked as a machinist, basically.
But we did all kinds of work-- sheet metal work, plumbing, and you name it, we did it.
For-- for when I worked for the other men who-- who when I lost my job with the [INAUDIBLE] construction, I made much less money.
I started from scratch as apprentice.
And I have papers here when they-- they-- they had, actually, big ceremony to-- to-- to turn-- to turn apprentice to journeymen, you know-- a graduation ceremony and so on.
They had their own guild of craftsmen there.
And they tried.
They tried to live as normal a life as possible.
And-- and we did.
Name was Joseph Schimel.
He was a man with little education, very smart.
He used to work for the Austrian railroad.
He liked the best to swing a hammer.
But he also had a good business sense.
He did fine.
He-- he-- what was needed at the beginning was petroleum kerosene stoves, wood burning stove.
And that's how he started out.
Later on, he went to the machine work and everything else-- sheet metal work.
My father and my mother did for-- for-- for-- as long as [INAUDIBLE] they did.
Basically, what they did was turning people like actors and people who still had to dress up and-- and-- the collars on a shirt goes first.
So what they did actually-- they cut from the bottom of the shirt off, then the tail, and made collars.
They had learned how to make custom shirts, actually, in Germany, but in a very rushed, short way.
And so-- so they did a little bit, did a little bit of-- made a little bit of money that-- that way for a while.

And later on, my father started to-- actually worked in a kitchen later on doing any kind of work in the-- in the kitchen of the-- where they had [INAUDIBLE] potatoes and cooking and so on [INAUDIBLE].
It was-- it was-- it was-- my father never got over-- he didn't get as bad or [INAUDIBLE] some other people.
But really, he did never get over that he was once thrown out of what he loved to do, and that was selling.
Selling-- not door-to-door type selling-- selling companies, you know?
He was not a salesman like the kind of sell-- maybe even insurance policies.
He-- his was on-- on a different level.
Because the people he talked to were-- were public relations officers of large companies.
In Frankfurt, they sent him to Berlin.
Berlin was mostly the headquarters of the big companies.
And that's when we moved-- why we moved to Berlin.
And he did very well.
I think he did probably 80% to 90% of the business that paper got was through him.
And he went to-- to-- to-- to these trade fairs, you know, and-- and-- and expositions and so on-- shows, radio shows, and so on-- and so on and so forth.
For them, in the different places like Leipzig, Frankfurt, and Berlin.
In Shanghai?
I must say, a lot of people were very positive.
And I have to take my hat off of all the actors and actresses.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's really amazing what they did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came to camps, just a couple of people-- maybe two or three-- and made us laugh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as they said later on, that was their pay.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They put up lavish performances.
They went up-- up to, like, operettas or operas.
I saw the opera [? common ?] in Shanghai there.
I didn't-- I really couldn't afford to see many. 'Merry Widower' I saw seven times because we ushering and helping out, and my boss was helping out with lighting and everything.
They used movie theaters.
In the-- in the restricted area, there were only two theaters in the restricted area we could use.
But even on-- in-- in the little community rooms we had-- like in the camp, there was the room [INAUDIBLE] people playing cards and so on.
They had a little stage set up [INAUDIBLE] plays.
I saw-- I saw many plays, mostly were done in German.
Because the-- the audience was German and-- and-- and the actors were German or Austrian.
There were-- a lot of them were not professional actors and some of them were professional actors.
One man I've seen before many times in Berlin in the Jewish theater, for instance.
Fritz [INAUDIBLE] was his name.
He was one of them.
He was also a cartoonist.
He was also a very good actor.
And I've seen 'Gypsy Baron,' 'Countess Maritza.' I don't know if they mean anything to you, and 'The Merry Widower.' I saw 'Carmen.' I-- I-- I saw more than that.
They had a regular theater that several theater groups, actually several.
There was one man, name was Karl [?
Border. ?] And I know a little bit about him because my mother was actually working-- working for him.
And his wife was Olga [INAUDIBLE], evidently Hungarian.
He was definitely Jewish.
I don't know if his wife was Jewish or not.
She was an opera singer.
And they did a lot of theater, propaganda theater, at the British embassy in Shanghai.
Or was it the British consulate?
I'm not sure if it was the consulate or embassy but-- I thought it was embassy.
Anyway, they had to show of their won, evidently.
I've never been there.
And we had some records.
They wanted some background music, and we borrowed them sometimes some records.
And only in the very last minute-- this group may have been as few as five, six.
It could have been as many as 10.
I don't know.
I only knew about him.
I didn't know him personally.
I've seen him and his wife but I-- on stage, but I didn't know him personally.
I only knew through-- my father knew him, of course.
And in the last minute, they got them out.
They would have been dead.
They did propaganda against the Nazis, and probably, I imagine, not too friendly towards the Japanese either.
That was before war in the Pacific broke out.
They got them out, that group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what happened to them.
No, most-- one of the-- one of the worst days I-- I experienced in Shanghai was also the most positive day.
It Was the 17th of July, 1945.
We were heavily bombed, American planes.
They tried to get an oil warehouse.
They didn't get it.
They got-- they got-- tried to get a Japanese radio station.
They missed it.
The Japanese radio station was right next to a camp.
And about-- I think about 60 of refugees got killed in that camp.
You can't find [INAUDIBLE].
We talked to the people later on that it's a bombing.
The way-- they explained how it is done, you cannot-- at that time, you couldn't pinpoint bomb.
I don't know you can do it today better, but probably.
But at that time, they-- they told us how that was.
And a lot of people-- a lot more Chinese got killed.
And we were organized.
We had to organize ourselves in air raid groups already.
And to be prepared for something like this, even though we-- we hoped not-- that would never happen.
Because before the bombing was usually warehouses and other strategic areas of Shanghai, never the area where we lived.
That was the only time.
Nearby, yes, but that was the only time it was really full hits.
And so I-- we were working.
And we were used to have-- the planes came daytime.
Those were the B-24s.
I think they flew high enough that the-- the Japanese anti-aircraft couldn't reach them.
Whether they were B-24s or B-29s, I'm not sure.
And they were very high and very small.
And when it hit there, we went out to the-- we were-- we were about eight or 10 of us all in our late teens-- mid- to late teens, I should say.
We went out.
And across the street, there was a first aid station in the camp.
And we got ourselves-- four of us got ourselves a carrier-- what do you call it?
You carry-- carry wounded people-- a carrier.
And then we went out and picked up some of the wounded.
And we brought them into the nearest hospital.
The nearest hospital was a jail hospital.
And they were totally unorganized.
There were hundreds of people there bleeding and wounded.
Some dragged themselves in there.
So we decided the next ones we pick up, we go to-- maybe half a mile away, there was a Japanese hospital outside the restricted area.
Well, we know about it.
But at that time, we didn't give a damn anymore.
We went.
And you know something?
When we carried the wounded people, there were barricades around-- around these Japanese areas, especially around the hospital.
The Japanese soldiers standing there opened the barricades for us.
That-- we didn't expect that.
They didn't expect us to be that charitable to other people, you see there.
The anti-Semitic propaganda worked pretty well with-- from the Nazis, you know.
It still works everywhere.
We know that.
And-- and-- but those soldiers-- like, unbelievable.
They were polite to us.
We brought them in there.
That Japanese hospital was damaged.
The other one was not.
The Japanese hospital was damaged but the people worked.
And I have to say that for them-- very [INAUDIBLE] people, as we know.
And they kept on doing their jobs.
It was damaged by the vibration.
Mainly cement and stuff came from the ceiling.
But people were working.
And we brought some wounded in there.
And then we didn't know that one of our camps was hit at that time.
And we-- we saw people on the street, Jewish doctors, Jewish girls, sawing off smashed legs and arms and limbs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was the worst and the best day because [INAUDIBLE] very proud of.
And the action of the Chinese was tremendous.
They brought food in our camp, themself being poor in our neighborhood.
They knew what we-- this day what the refugee community did as a community, mainly the younger people but also a lot of older ones.
Doctor, ?] any girl young girls-- yeah, they seen dead bodies.
But they never have worked on one and they had to.
On the street they set up.
Because if they saved some lives-- maybe not.
But they could have saved otherwise.
They-- they did with what little first aid they had to help anybody on the streets.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that you could be proud of.
And the Chinese did show appreciation very much that lived around us.
For-- we could hold our heads really up in the community.
And even the Japanese, they had cut our building off.
There was a little light shining out in the night.
They cut the power off in our building and turned it on again.
Because that building, the building I was living in, had-- had a clinic.
The first floor was occupied by-- by some people with TB and also a clinic was there, first aid clinic.
And they helped a lot of people.
And a lot of people died.
There were some-- I forgot how many.
But there may have been about 60 or so caskets on the soccer field back there.
[INAUDIBLE] casket [INAUDIBLE] Japanese or somebody brought them in and they picked them later on up.
People had died there, but they-- they didn't deny them first aid.
They gave them first aid.
They gave them whatever they could.
They saved probably a number of them.
A number of them they couldn't save anymore.
But I'd say I was proud of that day.
So as I say-- the worst day, was in the other respect the best day.
There was nothing best about it.

But you-- I-- I want to make it clear that it was for us in the community something to be proud of, especially since the Chinese by tradition, when you help somebody, save somebody's life, you're responsible for the rest of their lives.
Now that tradition was still going on with many Chinese.
Nevertheless, they understood us.
Nevertheless, they appreciated us.
And I also appreciate it from the Chinese that they appreciated us.
I have to say that for them, too, that they appreciated what we tried to do.

Because when you walk over rubble and see children's heads sticking out of rubbles and-- and people's heads and hair there, that what they did that day, this isn't-- this-- this is something you never forget.
What did I do after?
When the day was over in the afternoon, what I did, I didn't know anything else to do.
I laid on my back and took some junky book and started reading.
This is how I relieve myself of-- of the experience of that day.
But boy could we be proud of our young people.
Did I what?
Famous people?
Not really.
I can't see [? that ?] I meet.
I didn't even meet that Mr.
[INAUDIBLE].
I did not.
The people who went to school, of course, met him.
I didn't need any famous people from there.
There were some famous people among our refugees-- actors and mainly actors and-- and-- and people in-- in business that were at one time famous, yes.
But I didn't meet any famous people I can think of.
I saw [INAUDIBLE] well, oh yes.
Wait a minute.
[LAUGHS] I forgot General Wedemeyer.
You know who he was?
He was the commander of the American forces in China theater.
You heard of the Wedemeyer Report?
I think he had Alzheimer's.
He may be still alive, I don't know.
But I didn't meet him.
I was working.
I was working on a [INAUDIBLE] roof of a building the Red Cross put up.
This was after the war.
I worked for the army.
And he came into the room.
And I was working on the window, and he was there.
And the soldier [INAUDIBLE] was sitting there, shaving himself with something.
So he said that he wanted to get up and greet him.
Sit down, and he sat down right next to me where I was working.
He sat down and talked to the soldier-- where he was from, where his family was [INAUDIBLE].
He made a very nice impression.
So I didn't talk to him.
I talked to some other generals, but I did-- did not talk to General Wedemeyer.
He was right next under MacArthur.
When-- tell me what it was like for you when the war ended.
What happened at the camp?
